The meaning "measure of land" proposed for Syriac in LS2 is incorrect for the example cited there, though on the basis such a usage in Neo-Aramaic dialects and Arabic one may suppose that it did exist elsewhere.

entries.end_notes: The two nouns pdr #1 and #2 may well be the same word: DUNG, Bread or fat baked on Dung, and (mand) witchcraft. Note especially in Mandaic GY 74 where there is a word play between purda and hrara (the latter being "illusion" but sounding like "excrement"

See note DJBA for rejection of the meaning 'dung' and see note to pdr n.m. However, the DJBA argument is circular and to be rejected because in fact the word occurs as "dried dung" at Nid 28a(11) where DJBA has, however, emended it away to פרדא. Also see note to pdr #1. As shown by H. Mutzafi, Leshonenu 80:3(2018):290f., the form פידרא is also supported by Ma’lula Neo-Aramaic.
entries.extra_comments: DJBA s.v. פידרא. and also s.v. *prd) as emended !!! T 674 '; Heb. form also used in 71. but not in our lem files

entries.end_notes: Whether or not p_ is to be considered an authentic Aramaic conjunction or not (as opposed to the ubiquitous by-form ˀp, ˀwp) is uncertain. In OA texts from Sam'al it is surely a Samalian usage, while in the Middle Aramaic monumental dialects and Samaritan it reflects the common Arabic conjunction. Note that in those texts it reflects the Arabic semantics as opposed to the simple conjunctive waw.

entries.end_notes: This word occurs in primary texts only in the Peshitta where it "glosses" MT Job 40:17 פחדו, an equally obscure word, otherwise only in commentaries and lexical references to the passage. Although clearly to judge by the comparative Semitic evidence the Hebrew originally meant the thigh of an animal (see e.g. HALOT p. 757), later Jewish tradition uniformly understood it to be "testicles," (see s.v. pḥdyn ). Ephrem was familiar with this interpretive tradition, hence mng. 1. Later lexicographers follow meaning 2 for reasons of propriety. There is thus no justification to render this as "thigh" (as do LS2 and JPSm) in Syriac.

entries.form_section: For the "proper" difference in spelling between the ethpa and ettaph forms of this verb in Syriac see the discussion in A letter by Mār Jacob, bishop of Edessa, on Syriac orthography p. 6. JLATg ettaf )Tps ; CPA often bys
entries.end_notes: From Greek πεῖσαι < πείιθω. The foreignness of the initial peh is still felt Aramaic, hence the emphasis of the ṭ in the reflexive and the consistent hard pronunciation of the "p" in Syriac. The C stem usages reflect both the transitive and intransitive semantics of the Greek. See also note s.v. pss.

entries.end_notes:
The vocalization is based on the JBA and Mandaic spellings, as well as the Arabic loan pilizzun with final double consonant.
Since the etymology of this Mediterranean culture word is otherwise unknown, and the initial labial varies between /p/ and /b/, we would suggest an origin from a hypothetical Assyrian bu/illiltu, from the root balālu, "to mix;" compare billu A, "alloy, melt." For the phonological correspondences compare discussions s.v. mlš div. and zbn n.m.#2.

The second citation plibẖ taqata ḏ-br anašia (for which the correct reference is DC 45. 85:27) appears in an uncertain context, and its reading is not supported by the parallel in DC 46. 175:2, which reads gaiib liba taqata ḏ-br anašia ‘bends the heart … of men’. entries.end_notes: Greek ψαλτήριον, "psaltery, harp". Note that Syriac renders this as knr.

Compare Akkadian pasāsu, "to obliterate, cancel." Presumably this verb has disappeared from Syriac because of the near homonymy of its most frequent afel form with the extremely common borrowing from Greek -- pys vb. -- (with which pss #2 is also semantically related!), whereas in Jewish Aramaic dialects the latter appeared as a pael and thus remained distinct.

entries.end_notes: Old Persian. Flora 4:256. DJBA argues that in JBA the word almost always refers specifically to a vineyard as already attested in Neo-Babylonian Akkadian. But note that in the cited geonic passage (TGAs28(1) 192.1.21) the word is used both for an area producing a drink as well as grain. In Syriac, too, ܦܪܕܝܣܐ can be distinguished from ܓܢܬܐ, but whether always as a vineyard or generally an orchard rather than a vegetable garden for the latter, is not clear.

entries.end_notes:
DJPA treats the plural of this word simply as a form of pry in JPA (with much justification, also so for CPA!), but to judge by the existence of two clearly different forms of the singular in Syriac, as well as the appearance of clearly singular forms of this word in PTA, CPA, Mandaic and JBA, this has become a separate word in at least some if not most post-OfA dialects. In Babylonian it is probably the only form.
The situation is thus as follows: Prior to the later dialects, only pry, but unfortunately, there are no clear attestation of a plural before that time. In colloquial Palestinian dialects generally sg. pĕrī, sg. emph. pēryā, pl. pērīn with some contamination from Syriac and Babylonian in the texts; Syriac: both forms, but the meanings have been divided: this form is for the fruit of trees as well as figurative meanings while the form ܦܪܝܐ is used only in the figurative senses; Babylonian only pērā. LJLA is like Palestinian (i.e. sg. abs./constr=פרי) but only has pērā for the sg. emph. KWIC searches should examine both forms.

entries.end_notes: The targumist is being splendidly playful here, even more so than usual. By choosing this form, all of the following literary and lexical allusions are covered with one fell swoop: the source BH term ‏‏גַּנָּתוֹ and both its content and its similarity to גנון, "bridal chamber;" and the form פורינא, "bridal litter" and its BH cognate ‏אַפִּרְיוֹן and its context in Song of Songs 3:9. All of this play is echoed in (or echoes) PsJon Dt.32:50: אתקין ליה פוריינא וקטר ליה גננא בגויה. See also s.v. pwryn #2.

entries.primary_gloss: to supply, to support, to provide
entries.end_notes: The etymology of this verb and the many nouns related to it has defied scholarly attention. A Greek etymon seems almost inevitable, though, so we see no reason not to connect it with φερνίζω "to provide an endowment”. The recent article by A. Butts, "The Etymology of Aramaic (and Hebrew) √prns ‘to distribute, supply’”, JAOS 137(2017): 245ff. is totally unconvincing, for the initial consonant should correspond to Greek φ not π.

From Old Iranian *patigāma. On mng. 1a.1 cf. J. C. Greenfield 1982b.
In what is probably a majority of instances this lexeme has the connotation of an official or divine pronouncement as opposed to the otherwise unmarked mlh n.f.
Given the distribution of meanings, it is clear that the usage "thing, matter" in Jewish texts reflects the double meaning of BH דָּבָר.
